teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the lab workflow students are about to perform.
teaching_arc.progression: Move step by step through setup, execution, and checks without drifting into unnecessary theory.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a practical sequence they can follow during the lab.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Give students a reliable mental and procedural map for working through the Fanuc lab safely and efficiently.
topic_takeaways: Students should know the order of the main Fanuc lab steps.
Students should know what to verify before moving to the next step.
Students should understand the common procedural mistakes that slow labs down.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Use caution language around steps students often rush.
transition_style: Move from one lab checkpoint to the next, always explaining what students should confirm before continuing.

slides.narration_seed: Welcome to Lab 1. Safety is our first priority today.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Give students a reliable mental and procedural map for working through the Fanuc lab safely and efficiently.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quick Review: Robot Design so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame FANUC LR Mate 200iD as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep FANUC LR Mate 200iD connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how FANUC LR Mate 200iD sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Quick Review: Robot Design using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Articulated: Like a human arm (our Fanuc).
SCARA: Horizontal movement, very fast.
Delta: Parallel links for pick-and-place.
Cartesian: Linear X-Y-Z movement.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into The Big Three Safety Rules so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Quick Review: Robot Design first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Quick Review: Robot Design connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Quick Review: Robot Design clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Remind students: The robot is stronger than you and cannot feel your presence.
must_cover: Stay Outside: Never enter the reach of the robot while it is moving.
One Controller: The person holding the Teach Pendant is the ONLY person allowed inside the safety zone.
E-Stop Awareness: Always know where the nearest Emergency Stop is before turning the power on.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Quick Review: Robot Design into The Big Three Safety Rules so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into The Mechanical Unit: 6 Axes so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Explain The Big Three Safety Rules like an operating warning: state the risk, the consequence, and the safe rule.
slides.story_hint: Use a believable lab or shop-floor mistake rather than abstract warning language.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting The Big Three Safety Rules wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for The Mechanical Unit: 6 Axes using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Major Axes (J1, J2, J3): These position the robot in space.
Minor Axes (J4, J5, J6): These orient the wrist and the tool.
Each axis is driven by an AC Servo Motor.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Servos & Encoders so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for The Mechanical Unit: 6 Axes first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture The Mechanical Unit: 6 Axes clearly before the formal details continue.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Servos & Encoders using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Servo Motor: Converts electrical energy into precise motion.
Pulse Coder (Encoder): Attached to the motor; it counts rotations.
Closed-Loop: The controller constantly compares where the robot *is* vs. where it *should be*.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into The R-30iB Mate Controller so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for The R-30iB Mate Controller using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Main Breaker: Main power switch.
Mode Switch: T1 (Manual), T2 (Fast Manual), and AUTO.
Operator Panel: Contains the physical E-Stop and the Start button.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Inside the Controller so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Inside the Controller using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Servo Amplifiers: The 'muscles' that send high voltage to motors.
E-Stop Unit: A hardware-based safety circuit that bypasses software.
Main Board: The CPU that calculates kinematics and path planning.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for The Teach Pendant (iPendant) using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: USB Port: Used for backing up programs.
On/Off Switch: Must be ON for manual movement (T1 mode).
Display: 7-inch color screen showing status and code.

slides.narration_seed: Students often squeeze too hard when they panic. Remind them to find the 'soft middle'.
must_cover: Released (Pos 1): Robot is OFF.
Middle (Pos 2): Robot is ENABLED (Jogging allowed).
Fully Squeezed (Pos 3): Robot is OFF (Emergency Panic).
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from The Teach Pendant (iPendant) into The 3-Position Deadman Switch so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Navigating the Keypad so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Explain The 3-Position Deadman Switch like an operating warning: state the risk, the consequence, and the safe rule.
slides.story_hint: Use a believable lab or shop-floor mistake rather than abstract warning language.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting The 3-Position Deadman Switch wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Navigating the Keypad using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: COORD: Changes how the robot moves (Joint, World, Tool).
RESET: Clears error messages.
SHIFT: Must be held with other keys to perform actions.
+/- Override: Changes jogging speed.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: The next slide should compress the lesson into a quick memory or self-check.

slides.narration_seed: Use Startup Checklist as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: 1. Turn Main Breaker on the controller to the ON position.
2. Turn the Pendant Switch to ON.
3. Release all E-Stops (Twist to release).
Students should know the order of the main Fanuc lab steps.
4. Press RESET on the pendant (Red 'Fault' LED should go out).
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Startup Checklist to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into The Jogging Handshake so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Startup Checklist as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep Startup Checklist connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Startup Checklist with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for The Jogging Handshake using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: 1. Grip the Deadman switch in the middle position.
2. Hold the SHIFT key.
3. Press and hold a Blue Jog Key (J1-J6).
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Understanding COORD Modes using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: JOINT: Moves one motor at a time (e.g., only rotate base).
WORLD: Moves in a straight line relative to the center of the robot base.
Press the COORD key to toggle between these modes.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Reading the Screen using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Status Bar: Shows active mode (T1/AUTO) and speed percentage.
Software LEDs: BUSY (working), STEP (single line mode), FAULT (error active).

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Testing a Program using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: 1. Select your program from the SELECT menu.
2. Press STEP to toggle 'Step Mode' (one line at a time).
3. Hold Deadman + SHIFT.
4. Press FWD to execute the highlighted line.

slide_ref.source_title: Running a Program in AUTO Mode

slides.narration_seed: This follows the lab manual flow: AUTO mode disables pendant operation and runs from the controller START button. Remind students: ALWAYS validate in T1 step mode first, then switch to AUTO.
must_cover: 1. In T1, select the program and confirm it runs safely in STEP first.
2. Turn the controller key to AUTO (mode selector).
3. Release all E-Stops (controller and teach pendant).
4. On the pendant, switch the enable key to OFF (pendant is unavailable in AUTO).
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Safety Check so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
prop_suggestions: Controller Front Panel (Mode selector, START, E-Stop).
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Running a Program in AUTO Mode first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Running a Program in AUTO Mode connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Running a Program in AUTO Mode clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Treat Safety Check as a practical caution: explain the risk, what students often underestimate, and the safe operating rule they should remember.
must_cover: You are jogging the robot and it starts moving toward a table. You panic and squeeze the Deadman switch as hard as you can. What happens?
The 3-position deadman is designed to trigger an E-stop if you either release it OR squeeze it too hard in a panic.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Running a Program in AUTO Mode into Safety Check so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: The next slide should compress the lesson into a quick memory or self-check.
slides.explanation_style: Explain Safety Check like an operating warning: state the risk, the consequence, and the safe rule.
slides.story_hint: Use a believable lab or shop-floor mistake rather than abstract warning language.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Safety Check wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Use Hardware Check as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: Which axis is responsible for rotating the entire robot base left and right?
Joint 1 is the base axis for positioning the arm's orientation.
Students should know the order of the main Fanuc lab steps.
Students should know what to verify before moving to the next step.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Hardware Check to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by restating the destination of the topic: Students should finish with a practical sequence they can follow during the lab.
